behaviors.description: Images with explicit content are flagged by the AI vision model and held for human review
behaviors.justification: The core failure is that the real vision model fails to detect explicit content. A mock model returning flagged=True cannot catch this. Only calling the actual vision model reveals whether it correctly classifies explicit images, whether the threshold is appropriate, and whether the flag is properly persisted through the system.
behaviors.test_description: Submit an image containing explicit content through the moderation API, call the real vision model, and verify it returns flagged=True and the post is queued for human review (not published)

behaviors.description: Text model detects hate speech even with obfuscation techniques (letter substitution, unicode lookalikes)
behaviors.justification: The specific failure we must catch is: the real text model is fooled by obfuscation variants. A mock that always flags obfuscated hate speech cannot reveal this. Only the real model's classification logic will expose whether it actually resists adversarial input techniques.
behaviors.test_description: Submit multiple posts containing obfuscated hate speech variants (letter substitution, unicode lookalikes) and verify the real text model flags each variant as unsafe

behaviors.description: Posts passing both content checks are published rather than held or silently dropped
behaviors.justification: The failure is in orchestration logic (incorrect routing to wrong queue), not in model behavior. We can test this by mocking both models to return safe and verifying the post reaches the publish queue. Real models are not needed; the test verifies the decision logic and publish integration.
behaviors.test_description: Mock both vision and text models to return safe/not-flagged, submit a post through the pipeline, and verify it is routed to the publish queue (not held for review or dropped)

behaviors.description: Full moderation pipeline (image check, text check, publish decision) completes within 5 seconds
behaviors.justification: Latency depends on real AI model inference time, network I/O, and system load—all non-deterministic factors. A test with mocked instant-return models would always pass and hide real SLA violations. Only actual model calls reveal true end-to-end latency against the 5-second requirement.
behaviors.test_description: Measure end-to-end latency from post submission through moderation decision with real vision and text model calls, verifying completion within 5 seconds under normal load

behaviors.description: When the AI vision model is unavailable, image posts are queued for retry rather than auto-approved
behaviors.justification: The failure is in error handling and retry queue logic. We can catch this by mocking the vision model to fail (503/timeout) and verifying the post is queued for retry. The real vision API does not need to actually be down; simulating the failure via mock is sufficient to test retry behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the vision model API to return a failure (503 or timeout), submit an image post, and verify it enters the retry queue without being auto-approved

behaviors.description: Moderation decision for borderline images is consistent when submitted multiple times within a short time window
behaviors.justification: The primary failure is broken caching logic (re-processing instead of cached result), which causes inconsistency. This is testable at Integration level with mocked models. The phrase 'within short window' indicates caching is the core behavior. While model non-determinism could also affect borderline images, caching is the system's primary defense and is sufficient to catch the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Submit the same borderline image twice within 5 seconds and verify both submissions receive the same moderation decision; verify that caching is used to avoid re-processing
